slides.title: A second language *protects* your brain
slides.sub: Learning and using another language builds cognitive reserve. That buffer can delay dementia symptoms by years.

slides.title: Cognitive reserve, explained
slides.body: Reserve is the brain's spare capacity. The more you build, the longer it *holds the line* against age-related decline.

slides.title: A constant mental workout
slides.body: Switching between languages trains attention and control. That daily effort *can support* a more resilient, adaptable brain.

slides.title: Roughly four to five years
slides.body: Studies of bilingual adults link lifelong language use to dementia symptoms appearing *four to five years later*.

slides.title: It is never too late
slides.body: You do not need fluency or a childhood start. Learning a language at any age *may help* keep the mind sharp.

slides.title: A little, most days
slides.body: Short, consistent practice beats rare cramming. Pair it with the basics that protect your brain: sleep, movement and real food.

slides.title: Protect the *years that matter*.
slides.body: Vibe Health scores the sleep, food, movement and steps behind a sharper brain, all in one daily Health Score you can act on.
slides.cta_text: search ‘Vibe Health’ on the App Store

caption:
Learning a second language is one of the kindest things you can do for your future brain. 🧠🌿

It builds cognitive reserve, and lifelong bilingual use is linked to dementia symptoms arriving about four to five years later. Save this for your next study session. 🔖

Vibe Health turns the habits behind a sharper brain into one daily score → link in bio.
youtube_title: How a Second Language Protects Your Aging Brain
